title: Lesson 437
transcript: We just looked at some data destruction methods and how to prevent data remnants. Let's look at this in a little more detail. And one of the terms that typically comes up, certainly in the industry in relation to all of this, are these object reuse, object reuse. And I know the history of these terms here that you see from the NSA and also actually from NIST, the National Institute of Standards and Technologies. Basically, when they defined object reuse, if you looked at these two words, it tells you exactly what we're talking about here. We're talking about the reassignment of objects, specifically media. We're trying to reuse media. But what this means from a NIST perspective, and therefore what you might see on the exam, is that we are trying to reuse objects like media, for example, but while also ensuring that there is no residual data on that object because we're reusing it. So the idea is if that media contained something on it that was very sensitive or valuable, before we can reuse that media, that object, we have to securely get rid of what was on it so that there is no residual data that can be reconstructed. So when you see these two words, object reuse, it means really the secure reassignment of objects and media. And so typically the only way that you can get rid of residual data so that it cannot be reconstructed by any known means is to use overwriting techniques. Now I know we said that overwriting is really an example of clearing that data may not be reconstructed by any known means and certainly that's true as far as what the industry considers overwriting to be regardless of how many times you do it. All right. But if you're thinking about how we can reuse objects and media, the only option in most cases is to overwrite. And that's what object reuse actually means. So if you see these two words on the exam, just think overwriting. We're trying to reassign media. and objects in a secure way by getting rid of any residual data that may have been on that object by using overriding techniques. So just equate these together and if you see questions on the exam or answers on the exam that have these two words just focus on overwriting. Overwriting is what we're using in order to get rid of that residual data. Now, as far as secure reassignment and destruction techniques, obviously we talked about how to prevent data remnants. your best option is to destroy, destruction of the media. But if that's not available, then the next best option is purging. And we define purging as being something that will ensure that that data cannot be reconstructed by any known means. And then if that's not available, we have clearing technologies which may not allow the reconstruction. So we have newer technologies like flash technology, for example, how do we handle this requirement of preventing data remnants for technologies, newer technologies like solid state drives that do not use magnetic technologies to represent the bits, the zeros and the ones and the data, right? They use flash technology. So by definition, because it's not magnetic technology, There may not be any overriding capabilities built in, but obviously the makers of these technologies have thought about this need that we have to prevent data remnants. And so they will ship these technologies with capabilities to be able to do things like sanitization. And in many cases, crypto erase and crypto shredding. But your best option is always going to be destruction of the media itself, if that's available to you. And the way that pricing for technologies and media are today, in many cases, depending on the value, of the data that you're trying to get rid of, this could be a good option to destroy your hard drive or your solid state drives because it may still be cost effective depending on the value of the data that was on there. And just to re-emphasize, another option that you may have for environments where destruction is not available, like cloud computing, for example. So in cloud environments, unless you're using your own private cloud, that environment is not yours. And so destruction of the media may not be an option. And in those cases, the only option that you may have is what's called crypto shredding, crypto erase. And again, perfect terminology here and definition. You encrypt your data and then you securely and defensively destroy every copy of the key so that there is no opportunity for that data to be reconstructed. That's what crypto shredding, also referred to as crypto erase is. Not just throwing away the key, but securely and defensively destroying every copy of the key is what this is. And just one last time, destruction is your best option. So if you can take out the media or the object and physically destroy it, that's always going to be your best option according to the industry. Thank you.
